issue_comment_body: Thanks for adding these methods! I have just one question.

For the record, you should only assume a privmsg is a CTCP message if and only if `\x01` is the first  and last character of a `PRIVMSG`.

This is because there are actually multiple different types of these CTCPs, that fall under the banner of **"user requests"**:

`PRIVMSG target :\x01ACTION does something\x01` (/me does something)
`PRIVMSG target :\x01SED <ciphered message>\x01`
`PRIVMSG target :\x01CTCP VERSION\x01`
`PRIVMSG target :\x01CTCP PING\x01`

And so on.

For this reason, you should not blindly respond to any possible CTCP purely based on the presence of delimiters.

In addition, you are strongly discouraged from responding to any CTCP message sent to a channel automatically on practically every server in existence.

There is no "spec", as such, because CTCP/DCC isn't actually in the IRC spec, but the beginning and end of a PRIVMSG if the delimiter `\x01` is at both the start and end of a line, you should attempt to parse it as a CTCP message.

From [The only really "available" spec](http://www.irchelp.org/irchelp/rfc/ctcpspec.html), which isn't the original, this is how you embed multiple CTCP messages inside one.

>     M = ctcpQuote(H1) || '\001' || ctcpQuote(X) || '\001' || ctcpQuote(H2)

aka:

`PRIVMSG target :\x01MYFUNC \x01\x01\x01SECONDFUNC\x01\x01\x01THIRDFUNC\x01`

The [original spec, from the wayback machine](https://web.archive.org/web/20101129023108/http://www.invlogic.com/irc/ctcp.html#4) (Section 4) is actually a lot more explicit about how user requests work, and shows exactly why you cannot embed a CTCP VERSION or other user request inside of a normal message:

> The basic format of any request will be as follows:
>
>     PRIVMSG <space> <target> <space> : <marker> <command> [<arg> [...]] <marker>
>
> Within the framework of these requests, some may generate a response. This response will take the following general form:
>
>     NOTICE <space> <target> <space> : <marker> <command> [<arg> [...]] <marker>
>
> Requests which are not recognised or are invalid may return an error message similar to the following:
>
>     NOTICE <space> <target> <space> : <marker> ERRMSG \ <command> [<arg> [...]] <marker>

The **real** reason for allowing embedded CTCP inside messages is for test formatting (Section 3 of the above spec).

I can send the following to a channel and clients that understand it would parse this:

    PRIVMSG target :Hello, this is \x01Uunderlined!\x01, \x01Iitalic\x01 and \x01Bbold!\x01

as:

> Hello, this is \_underlined!\_, *italic* and **bold!** (note: markdown doesn't have underlining)

I did some more checks, and it appears it doesn't matter if you call `call_sync` or `call_with_unix_fd_list_sync`, since they both call `g_dbus_connection_call_sync_internal` anyway. (https://git.gnome.org/browse/glib/tree/gio/gdbusconnection.c?h=2.50.0#n6162 vs https://git.gnome.org/browse/glib/tree/gio/gdbusconnection.c?h=2.50.0#n6281)

The question now is how to solve this in the cleanest way. I would propose to use `call_with_unix_fd_list_sync` either way, since it works the same for either case. Only matter now is deciding how to deal with the return value in the best way. I think the best result would be to copy the result of dbus-python, and replace the return values with the file descriptor as an integer.

It turns out that it will not be as straight forward to add as I had originally thought. It does not integrate well into the unit formatting framework in currently `pint` (in `formatting.py`). The issue is that `pint` formats the magnitude and units separately and the functions in `formatting.py` just handle the formatting of units. An `siunitx` formatter needs access to bot the magnitude and unit of a quantity in order to put them into an `\SI{mag}{unit}` command.

So, the `__format__` functions in the `quantity` and `measurement` classes (and probably the `unit` class as well) need to be modified to handle this as a special case. I am currently using a monkey patch and have been testing for about a month now without problems.

I will be happy to integrate this into the library and submit a PR if @hgrecco is interested.

You are looking at a chimeric alignment, which is caused by a common artifact in PacBio reads. Here, the first half of the read is approximately the reverse complement of the second half. Minimap2 aligned the two parts to the same chromosomal position but onto the opposite strands.

Like bwa-mem, minimap2 applies hard clipping to the second half of read. With hard clipping, column 10 only shows the sequence in alignment (i.e. it is a subsequence), not the entire sequence.

Just one critical note, I'm not sure I like the factory function for `ChainedMinimizer`, wouldn't it make more sense to just have `__init__` accept a `minimizers` (keyword) argument? e.g.

```python
def f(x, a, b):
    return a * x + b

def chi_squared(a, b):
    return np.sum((ydata - f(xdata, a, b))**2)

custom_minimize = ChainedMinimizer(chi_squared, [a, b], minimizers=[DifferentialEvolution, SLSQP])
fit_results = custom_minimize.execute()
```

I think this looks cleaner than

```python
CustomMinimizer = ChainedMinimizer([DifferentialEvolution, SLSQP])
custom_minimize = CustomMinimizer(chi_squared, [a, b])
```

And I wrote `fit_results` in plural because I think it would help users for debugging purposes if this method returned all the `FitResults`, as a list.

As some final icing on the cake, we can have `Fit` accept a list for the `minimizer` argument, which we will then simply wrap internally. This would then for most use cases reduce the above code to

```python
x, y = variables('x, y')
a, b = parameter('a, b')

model = {y: a * x + b}
fit = Fit(model, x=xdata, y=ydata, minimizer=[DifferentialEvolution, SLSQP])
fit_results = fit.execute()
```

`minimizer_kwargs` could then be passed as
```python
fit_results = fit.execute(minimizer_kwargs=[{'popsize': 100}, {'tol': 1e-9}])
```

The good news is that the changes appear to be fixes to the documentation in a few places.

Design.

The cache.clear() method can simply blow the cache away (current behavior) or it can evict each entry calling the callback function, if there is one. It is not obvious, at least to me, that either behavior is clearly more intuitive.

At a practical level, the WriteBackCacheManager class gives an example of the tradeoffs that motivated the API's design.

The clear() method there needs to clear the cache and the backing storage. Since the storage is being cleared anyway, we don't want the callbacks executing, we really want to blow the cache away.

In the sync() method we need to write back the dirty cache entries. If cache.clear() did called the callbacks, it would make this simpler. However, the performance would not be as good, because every entry in the cache would have its callback executed so that it could be looked up in the dirty set. For many cases, it is faster to just walk the dirty set directly, sync those, then blow the cache away.

The new logic is basically doing:

* Cast rays bidirectionally (along a random vector, rather than the Z axis to avoid models with features along Z). 
* If the hit count agrees in both directions, then we use that result. 
* If one of the directions hits nothing, we declare the point in free space even if the counts disagree. I haven't seen an edge case where we miss hits yet, but this step is pretty arbitrary and may need revisiting later
* If the two directions disagree and the point is not in free space, we try a contains test again for those points (but only once to avoid infinite recursion) along a random vector

If cache as a function would be in `__call__` it would executed each time the cache is requested. Or there should be some check whether we already executed and getted the cache. In `__init__` we just initializing the value one time.

I don't think that users could easily call the function and pass the value to the decorator, as it evaluated at class/method creation. So it would not be possible to dynamically configure cache at runtime. With function as argument it is possible. Actually it was my naive approach to implement `cachedmethod` pattern with access to cache with `operator`:
```python
from boltons.cacheutils import LRU,cached
import operator

class ClassWithCache(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.cache = LRU()

    @cached(operator.attrgetter('cache'))
    def number(self, value):
        return value

instance = ClassWithCache()
print(instance.number(19))
```

But as I discovered, it requires a lot more implementation, so I created #70 :)

`nbtlib` currently uses big-endian byte ordering, which is the format used by the original version of Minecraft. I know that versions of Minecraft based on the pocket edition use little-endian ordering, so making it possible to choose the endianness when reading and writing nbt data is on my todo list.

However, `nbtlib` is not a library for working with Minecraft files specifically. It's an implementation of the official nbt specification. Nbt data is used in a lot of different ways, and it's often integrated within other file formats. This library is supposed to let you read sections of files that contain spec-conforming nbt data. It won't work out of the box on files that do not contain raw nbt data, such as the Minecraft Education `level.dat` file since it has a header that you'll need to parse yourself before getting to the section that contains actual nbt data.

```
[root@localhost ~]# ip link show dev dummy0
16: dummy0: <BROADCAST,NOARP> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue state DOWN mode DEFAULT group default qlen 1000

Two discussions need to be had:

We can't use STL functions that raise exceptions without catching them and converting them.  I try to use a safe subset of C++ that isn't going to raise unexpected exceptions.  Otherwise we're going to get a crash instead of a clean exit with a Python exception.  Perhaps that is what is going on with MySQL.  Can this be done with more or less straight C?

Second, the documented ODBC interface for intervals is here: [Interval Data Types](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms716506(v=vs.85).aspxhttps://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms716506(v=vs.85).aspx).  We should be calling GetData with a SQL_INTERVAL_STRUCT and returning an error if a driver doesn't support it.

I'm not clear where the format that the `read_time_token_list` function is reading is coming from.  If it is just what one DB happens to read when asking for data as a string, it won't work across databases.

Thoughts?

If you choose to do convert from unsigned to signed and back outside of smbus2, you could consider to take the brute-force route and use the [ctypes module](https://docs.python.org/2/library/ctypes.html). Quick'n dirty but it should work OK.

```
from ctypes import c_int8, c_uint8

....
# Converting unsigned byte read to signed
unsigned_byte = bus.read_byte_data(80, 0)  # Assume read value is 200
signed_byte = c_int8(unsigned_byte).value  # signed_byte becomes -56

....

# Convert signed byte to unsigned before write
signed_byte = -123
unsigned_byte = c_uint8(signed_byte).value  # 133
bus.write_byte_data(80, 0, unsigned_byte)
```

You can do the same with c_int16 and c_uint16, but here, as you say, little/big endian comes into play as well.

The `events()` method returns a Python generator, so the short answer is "no". You'd need a separate thread that consumes from the SSE event stream in a blocking way, and either emits the events via a callback, or puts them into a queue:

```python
import threading
import sseclient

def consume(url, callback):
    for e in sseclient.SSEClient(url).events():
        callback(e)

# callback can be whatever, or it can be something that puts into a shared queue:
def callback(e):
    q.offer(e)

t = threading.Thread(target=consume, args=(url, callback))
t.start()
```

At least I tried to get the code with threads working. After a lot of fiddling with the producer-consumer pattern it works. The producer can be notified when the generator was closed and finish the thread. So it is possible to break from within the for loop and the producer thread finishes correctly. The trick to coordinate the consumer and producer is to use two singleton queues (one for requests and the other for responses).

A generator can either finish correctly or be closed. Eg. when break occurs before the generator completes. In this case we can signal to the producer, that it should finish. The failing test case was a bit strange usage since since it neither completely iterated the generator not closed it. It would correspond to a situation when processing one item hangs. A possible behavior is to finish the producer thread after some time of consumer inactivity.

You are correct, these examples need `methods=['GET','OPTIONS']` to work.
It was omitted because at one stage I had the `automatic_options` configuration default to `True`.

If you are using the extension method (`CORS(app)`), rather than the decorator _and_ you have `automatic_options=True` in the CORS config, then you _don't_ need to add  `methods=['GET','OPTIONS']` because OPTIONS is handled in a sanic request middleware before it gets to your route.

However in the case of the examples in the README, `automatic_options` is set default which is `False` so it _does_ need the change here.

hey @kavdev, I found some time to take a look at this, and I've got a couple things that I'm leaning towards to. Happy to discuss if you disagree on some aspects!

*The `ChoiceItem` modifications:*

1. I think we shouldn't just `setattr` the custom properties, I'd rather keep them a bit 'protected' in a `self._extras` attribute. I think this doesn't pollute the namespace too much, and it makes it easier to keep track on what exactly was passed in as custom props (and useful for item 2.). So the init would become more like this:

    ```python
        def __init__(self, value=sentinel, label=None, order=None, **extra):
            self.value = value
            self.label = label
            self._extra = extra

            if order is not None:
                self.order = order
            else:
                ChoiceItem.order += 1
                self.order = ChoiceItem.order
    ```

2. Since the introduction of arbitratry props may complicate things, I think it'd be wise to implement the `__repr__` method to make debugging easier:

    ```python
        def __repr__(self):
            extras = " ".join([
                "{key}={value!r}".format(key=key, value=value)
                for key, value in self._extra.items()
            ])
            return "<{} value={!r} label={!r} order={!r}{extras}>".format(
                self.__class__.__name__, self.value,
                self.label, self.order,
                extras=" " + extras if extras else ""
            )
    ```

*The DjangoChoices class:*

Essentially this would be 'opening up' the `_fields` api, but I'd make it a class method:

```python
    @classmethod
    def get_choice(cls, value):
        """
        Return the underlying :class:`ChoiceItem` for a given value.
        """
        attribute_for_value = cls.attributes[value]
        return cls._fields[attribute_for_value]
```

The details are in the `cls.attributes`, which _may_ throw an exception if duplicate values are used.

Example usage would then be:

```python
>>> choice_item = MyChoices.get_choice(MyChoices.foo)
```

Thoughts?

Thanks for the comments, @hiccup7! Good to have some input from experience. Here are some thoughts (mostly for reference for when I look at this again)... 
* Since the Python's wave module supports 24-bit, it would make sense to work toward support of native 24-bit playback. At this point, it would probably start with an optimistic attempt to play and error of the OS doesn't support it. 
* Native floating point doesn't seem worth the effort at this point, especially since actual hardware (DAC) support is probably quite rare/expensive. However, an independent module could handle reading such files at the binary level and turn them into objects in a similar manner to the wave module that can be bit-crushed or otherwise processed. 
* It might be worth looking at including an additional utility submodule (or maybe just a separate package) to do sample-size conversion. That way the playback interface can still have sample size explicitly specified but handling multiple formats with limited audio interfaces would be easier.

Finalizing on the "_select() returning a string", I'd say that if 
q = db(db.table.id>0)._select()
db.executesql(q[:-1])
works with what you want to make _select() do, we should be fine.

If we want to really save time, it should be saved in avoiding expanding arguments by ourselves, as the 99% of the drivers are mature enough to do it by themselves (and query plans for prepared statements are cached on the backend, that's one real speedup).  It'll eventually close also the requirement for using binaries instead of base64 encoding them (and all the memory issues in heavy-processing pieces of code).

@mstokes42 If your confusion matrix is representative of your test/train set, it looks like you're using very few (~ 100) training examples with what appears to be LeNet-5. This is about 2 orders of magnitude too little data; in [Lecun98](http://vision.stanford.edu/cs598_spring07/papers/Lecun98.pdf) LeNet-5 was trained using MNIST (60k 32x32 training images).

The reason you're getting 100% accuracy on your train set is that LeNet-5 is memorizing your training data and not learning enough to generalize to your test set. 

Take a look at the Convolutional Neural networks section for some examples with training sets:
https://gluon.mxnet.io/chapter04_convolutional-neural-networks/cnn-scratch.html
E.g.
`
batch_size = 64
num_inputs = 784
num_outputs = 10
def transform(data, label):
    return nd.transpose(data.astype(np.float32), (2,0,1))/255, label.astype(np.float32)
train_data = gluon.data.DataLoader(gluon.data.vision.MNIST(train=True, transform=transform),
                                      batch_size, shuffle=True)
test_data = gluon.data.DataLoader(gluon.data.vision.MNIST(train=False, transform=transform),
                                     batch_size, shuffle=False)
`
Additionally, if this is the issue, please feel free to close the issue. Thanks!

This case was easy to reproduce.
Step 1, kill the celery broker.
Step 2, start the scheduler(celery executor), make sure tasks will be call apply_async
Step 3, start celery broker, new tasks was fine to executed,  but the tasks called by apply_async in step 2 will be lost and never be executed.
Step 4, because of "running" queue, restart scheduler will make the lost tasks apply_async again and be executed.

Getting an signature_invalid error when working with SmugMug and using a query string in the request.

Requests work well with SmugMug API (api.smugmug.com) except when a query is added.

This example works: https://api.smugmug.com/api/v2/node/GS0g9!children

This example fails: https://api.smugmug.com/api/v2/node/GS0g9!children?start=11&count=10

The string built within session is 
  `https://api.smugmug.com/api/v2/node/GS0g9!children`**&count=10**`?oauth_consumer_key=...?oauth_nonce=...?oath_signature_method=...?oauth_timestamp=...?oauth_token=...?oauth_version=...`**?start=11**

Note the first part of the query string "?start=11" is moved to the end of the Uri yet "&count=10" is left in place.

The failed example is created when calling session.get("https://api.smugmug.com/api/v2/node/GS0g9!children?start=11&count=10",headers={'Accept': 'application/json'})

The same call without the query string "?start=11&count=10" works.

Nope. You need to catch both.

In 2.6 dns problems, connection problems, socket timeouts are all urllib2.URLError, so it was possible to catch only that one.

In 2.7 and later dns problems, connection problems are still URLError, while timeouts are socket.timeout

```
$ cat timeout.py 
#!/usr/bin/env python
from __future__ import print_function

from platform import python_version
from six.moves.urllib.error import URLError
from six.moves.urllib.request import urlopen
import socket

try:
    response = urlopen("http://localhost:8000/", timeout=1)
except (URLError, socket.timeout) as e:
    print("{0} URLError or socket.timeout: {1}".format(python_version(), type(e)))
```

Start a never answering tcp connection somewhere like:

```
$ while true; do nc -l 8000; done
```

and try it with different versions of python to see the exception for a timeout:

```
2.6.9 URLError or socket.timeout: <class 'urllib2.URLError'>
2.7.6 URLError or socket.timeout: <class 'socket.timeout'>
3.4.3 URLError or socket.timeout: <class 'socket.timeout'>
```

Kill the nc and see what happens with a connection problem.

```
2.6.9 URLError or socket.timeout: <class 'urllib2.URLError'>
2.7.6 URLError or socket.timeout: <class 'urllib2.URLError'>
3.4.3 URLError or socket.timeout: <class 'urllib.error.URLError'>
```

six already takes care about urllib2 vs urllib.error for us.

I think we can improve on this.  Just brainstorming an idea here:

We have to keep in mind that the methods available on resources/clients are generated at runtime, which would mean that the exceptions would have to be generated at runtime as well.  One thing we could do is attach this to the client objects (and similarly for resources).  It could look something like this:

```
try:
    client.create_user(...)
except client.exceptions.EntityAlreadyExists:
    handle_error()
except client.exceptions.NoSuchEntity:
    handle_other_error()

The main question is that should these keywords in general work with non-string values. This has been discussed lately with another kw (don't remember which) that used to work with non-strings but due to changes in v3.0 didn't anymore. Changing any single kw is easy, but it would be inconsistent if all aren't changed. That's obviously a rather big task.

If we'd decide to change all kws, then the code should handle all non-string values. Handling only floats, not even ints, would be inconsistent.

If this was to be implemented, it would make sense to do it in generic manner and somehow automatically. In practise that would mean doing it on Robot's side before calling the keyword. It would be great to use type annotations to tell Robot which argument type are expected, but because that's Python 3 feature it's not possible with SL that needs ti support Python 2 as well. Probably could use `@keyword` decorator for that somehow. Anyway, this is an issue to Robot itself.

Thanks for the replies so far. I'm a bit concerned about this strategy. 

While I can understand the pragmatic benefits (and the necessity for the `patched`) it is a bad idea for many reasons. First of all, if other packages would follow this example we'll soon have tons of duplicates of packages in a single package. It is starting already with pipenv, which in turn vendors pip which in turn vendors packages that have already been vendored by pipenv:

```bash
$ tree -d -L 3 pipenv/vendor/
pipenv/vendor/
├── backports
│   ├── shutil_get_terminal_size
│   └── weakref
├── blindspin
├── click
├── click_didyoumean
├── colorama                 # <<<
├── iso8601
├── jinja2
├── Levenshtein
├── markupsafe
├── pexpect
├── pip9
│   ├── commands
│   ├── compat
│   ├── models
│   ├── operations
│   ├── req
│   ├── utils
│   ├── vcs
│   └── _vendor
│       ├── cachecontrol
│       ├── colorama         # <<<
│       ├── distlib
│       ├── html5lib
│       ├── lockfile
│       ├── packaging
│       ├── pkg_resources
│       ├── progress
│       ├── requests         # <<<
│       └── webencodings
├── pipreqs
├── ptyprocess
├── pytoml
├── requests                 # <<<
│   └── packages
│       ├── chardet
│       └── urllib3
├── requirements
├── shutilwhich
└── yarg
```
pipenv provides the requests and the colorama package **twice**. Additionally, almost all packages from `pipenv/vendor/pip9/_vendor` are also in `pipenv/patched/notpip/_vendor`. 

The problems will start once you need to update one of your dependencies (e.g. a security issue in the requests package) -- in a normal situation you'd just update the library and all depending packages benefit automatically, but with this setup this is impossible as you have to keep track of **all** your vendors (and vendors vendors!).

Space is another issue, several versions of the same lib installed by the same package is a waste of space.

And last but not least. This is the Python Packaging Authority right? So a lot of people look at this code and will take it as an example or best practice -- currently we might not provide the PyPA the best service.

I do respect the author's decision for vendoring, but if you'd be willing to replace the unpatched vendored packages with normal (i.e. `install_requires`) dependencies, I'll gladly help and provide patches.

> @rgommers This is just by curiosity, but why not move distutils fixes and additions from ``numpy.distutils`` to ``setuptools``? Your fixes and new feature may be useful also for non Numpy users. And this may also avoid this kind of problem if future.

It's actually a similar question as "why don't we put all of ``distutils`` in ``setuptools``? ``distutils`` is badly maintained, and it really doesn't belong in the stdlib (except the parts needed to build Python itself).

#### I found another problem that the bytes sequence may contain unprintable characters

`wcwidth` think these characters are unprintable:
https://github.com/jquast/wcwidth/blob/c71459ea91af86f3bbcdac2c8ed5e7773da2d848/wcwidth/wcwidth.py#L175-L176

When pyte receives an unprintable character, it doesn't draw it on the buffer:
https://github.com/selectel/pyte/blob/676610b43954b644c05823371df6daf87caafdad/pyte/screens.py#L522-L523

As a result, following characters would never be drawn:
https://gist.github.com/eight04/dd7511c289d83932d18d17e21734bab3

-----

We need a flag to put unprintable bytes to the buffer.

Hi,

I think you want to use `where`.  It uses the same conventions as `numpy.where`:

It turns out this is only an issue with `eventlet` installed. Importing `raven` causes all of the transports to get imported, including https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/356a7c4d83a5289e7b30a07b0f76829e274b7481/raven/transport/eventlet.py#L14
which eventually imports https://github.com/eventlet/eventlet/blob/49773bb12b84e10ccb0a199c301f97fd6528ab68/eventlet/green/httplib.py#L16
which causes the check here to fail https://github.com/jcgregorio/httplib2/blob/cf631a73e2f3f43897b65206127ced82382d35f5/python3/httplib2/__init__.py#L1366
because httplib2 imported `http.client` before it was replaced by an identical version by eventlet. The simple fix is to import `raven` before importing `httplib2`/`twilio`.

Identifying all `expected` leaves or properties sounds rather easy. > type -> unit > format -> format Here is the list of all values and their repartition: ``` 4 format: bool 401 format: float 152 format: integer 952 format: percent 25 type: age 1 type: days 1 type: hours 496 type: monetary 1 type: months ``` (obtained via `xsltproc extract-formats.xsl openfisca_france/parameters/*.xml | sort | uniq -c`, the XSLT stylesheet being [here](https://gist.github.com/MattiSG/8df5f5044faf0cb6133b109f8f8ef731); I checked results count consistency with `grep format | wc -l`) I thus see that the types `days`, `hours` and `months` are used each only once. I think this leads us to decide whether type information, which is a recurrent documentary need, should be described as normalised metadata or be put in the description. Here is the current usage: ```xml ``` As one can see, both description and metadata is used, and I would see a benefit in including the type in the variable name itself. As a maintainer, I don't see the value of the `type` attribute in this context, as I already know the value is in hours from the description (and, possibly, from the variable name). As a reuser, I could see the value of being able to suffix the value with a unit to present it to the user. However, maintaining a mapping of types to suffices sounds like a never-ending task to me. ## Question What is the current use of the `format`? Is it simply exposed to the outside as part of documentation, or does it trigger some special handling, such as dividing a `percent` by 100?

Sorry for remissing a huge potential risk (silent failing) that breaks the gradient: some nodes need the original forward value to calculate the gradients (like tanh), thus they cannot be rewritten by any inplaced operators (in simple nodes we can specifically restore the values, but this might not be general and efficient). Really need to carefully inspecting this feature about inplacing operations ...

Thus adding a method `set_rewritable` to explicitly declare that the expression(node) gives up its memory. This could also be used for all the nodes in testing mode since we do not need to calculate gradients. Unfortunately, this has to be done by the users. (currently all the nodes set rewritable=false by default, thus `set_rewritable` needs to be called before all inplaced operations, but this could be further relaxed for nodes that are safe.)

Here is a simple python MLP code for testing inplaced-tanh:

    import dynet as dy
    import random, sys
    if sys.argv[1].startswith("in"):
        inplaced = True
        print("inplace", file=sys.stderr)
    else:
        inplaced = False
        print("notinplace", file=sys.stderr)
    Ni, Nh, BS = 5, 5, 1
    m = dy.Model()
    random.seed(12345)
    ini1 = [([random.random() for _1 in range(Ni)], 1) for _ in range(BS)]
    ini0 = [([random.random() for _1 in range(Ni)], 0) for _ in range(BS)]
    ws = { "w1": m.add_parameters((Nh, Ni)), "b1": m.add_parameters((Nh,)), "w2":m.add_parameters((1, Nh)), "b2":m.add_parameters((1,))}
    ps = {}
    for k in ws:
        ps[k] = dy.parameter(ws[k])
    tr = dy.SimpleSGDTrainer(m)
    losses = []
    for _ in range(100):
        for x, y in ini0+ini1:
            h0 = dy.inputVector(x)
            h1 = dy.affine_transform([ps["b1"], ps["w1"], h0])
            h1.set_rewritable(True)
            h1t = dy.tanh(h1, inplaced=inplaced)
            h1d = dy.dropout(h1t, 0.2)
            out = dy.affine_transform([ps["b2"], ps["w2"], h1d])
            losses.append(dy.square(y-out))
        loss = dy.esum(losses)
        loss.backward()
        tr.update()
    for w in sorted(ws.keys()):
        print("%s: %s" % (w, ws[w].as_array()))

After calling it with inplaced(`sys.argv[1].startswith("in")`) or not-inplaced(others), the same weights should be obtained.

I think we have enough here to move forward. The short version is:

- If someone tries to use this with Python 2.6, we should show them a warning saying "you really shouldn't be doing this".
  - If they find bugs that are easy to fix for Py26, we should give them a fair shake, but can't promise to deal with everything.
  - When we have to choose between Py26 and Py27, we go with Py27
- Testing dependencies that "would go away by dropping Py26 support" probably shouldn't get chopped "just because we can". Adding an extra dep really isn't that big of a deal.
- If possible, it'd be nice to keep running Py26 tests, so that we can fix things in the spirit of the first item on this list.

Otherwise, I think it's fine to say "No more Python 2.6 as a first-class citizen" (App Engine only talks about Python 2.7 in their docs now).

@albypeace, thank you! I like the approach to limit the connections to the proxy port, however, please, keep in mind that the value 32 that you used in the example is too low. Having one computer that uses the ShadowProxy does not mean that there will be only 1 connection. Each connection that the computer makes is counted. For example, browsers usually have a limit of 16 connections per domain. As a result, if you will open 4 tabs in browser that uses ShadowProxy, then you may have up to 64 connections already! If you use torrents, then you may have hundreds of connection, for example, uTorrent by default allows up to 200 connections. You can check the number of connections on the server using the following command:
``
netstat -anp | grep :${SHADOWSOCKS_PORT} | grep ESTABLISHED | wc -l
``

I suggest to put a value about 1000 or even 10000 per user per IP for limiting the connections. Per user means, that if you have 5 users who use the same port and share the same IP, then you should put value `5 x 10000 = 50000`. So, for one user it will be something like:
``
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport ${SHADOWSOCKS_PORT} -m connlimit --connlimit-above 10000 -j REJECT --reject-with tcp-reset
``

Besides, may be it will be a better idea to use `-j DROP` instead of `-j REJECT`? Can you please explain why you prefer `REJECT`?

Agreed re not worrying about different output on cold vs. hot runs. 

However it is generally desirable to capture tool logging/console output in a more principled way than we currently do, so that it's always available in a well-known `.pants.d` location. But that's future work, obvi.

I'm also not sure how they could fit together -- I was just thinking since both are (in a vague sense) about finding some resource matching some address spec (which is either a path or a target glob), it could make sense to (as you said) report the errors similarly, especially since these should probably (I think) both be checked after parsing BUILD files, but before executing anything.

1) Re "If they are used in the idea goal, why couldn't they be in a plugin?", I would say - because code in pantsbuild/pants can naturally use all sorts of code that we don't want outside code to use, for the obvious reason that when hacking on that code, the scope of testing is entirely within the pants repo. 

That's not an argument for why these specific names should be private, of course, but it does counter the claim that anything used in any internal task can be used externally. If we want that to be true (and I guess it's not totally unreasonable) then we have to decide on that as a group, and we have to enforce that no internal task code ever uses any private API. I.e., that tasks, even internal ones, are treated as-if they are external. 

So I guess my question is: can you upstream your `idea` reimplementation and solve this for now? :)


2) Not including safe_mkdir was an oversight. It should also be private, methinks. Is it useful? Yes. Should Pants be a repository of useful things to be used wherever? Maybe, but we have to all agree on that, and recognize that it hugely impacts our surface area and makes changes harder. Ditto, temporary_dir, assert_list et al. These are tiny, easy to replicate functions that I would probably prefer people implemented in their own repos as needed.

I would argue that setting night mode without playing TV is a valuable feature. If one wants to automate night mode (e.g. every week-day after 23), it would be irritating to have to change the source to TV, enable night mode, then set the source back again.

I instead created a is_playbar method that will check the model_name of the speaker. This will not send NightMode packets to the Sonos if it is not identified as a Playbar. I find this to be a better solution compared to sending incompatible packets to Sonos devices and responding to the Invalid Args exception afterwards, as we decrease unnecessary traffic on the network.

I'm fine with `-r` as a shorthand for `--recursive`, but `-f/--ignore-missing` seems like a very odd combination.

`-f` is often the short form of `--force`, which can mean all kinds of things. `rm -f` has quite different meaning from `globus delete --ignore-missing`, and I worry the mismatch could produce confusion.
(Yes, `man rm` makes it sound like `-f` skips missing files as its primary function, but skipping prompts is actually hugely important.)
The most comparable option on a well-known command is probably `--ignore-existing` on `rsync`, which has no short-form.

We can do it, but I want us to make sure that's actually the right behavior.

I see 3 different ways to implement the TV version
* Using the prox_tv is probably the easiest way to implement it. However, it results in an algorithm with two levels of iterations.
* Using the solver `mlfbf` of the pyunlocbox with the `norm_l1` of the pyunlocbox. This will result in an algorithm that has only one iteration. However, `mlfbf` is not the best solver for that problem.
* Implementing Chambolle-Pock algorithm in the pyunlocbox and using it with the `norm_l1`. Probably the best way, but also require more work... (https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-00490826/document)

With regards to your timestring, it's only a regex.  Without providing any additional filters than timestring, you're effectively sending a matching regex like: `^.*\d\d\d\d.*$`, and looking for 4 numbers in a row.  This is why it's matching any index with 4 numbers in a row.  Though you've only provided `%Y`, you are specifying `days` as your time unit.  However, there is nothing currently checking to ensure that you have a `day` in your timestring.  This is also why you're picking up indices much newer than the 365 day cutoff, because your timestring specifies only years, but you want to filter by days.

With regards to Kibana indices, Curator only prunes the 3 default Kibana index names: `.kibana` (K4), `kibana-int` (K3), and `.marvel-kibana` (Marvel).  Thus `kibana-int-foo` is not a match.  This is documented [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/delete.html#_summary_34) in the Note: section.

That said, I cannot understand why `kibana-int-foo` is even matching your search criteria at all.  If you run with `--debug`, it will provide much more insight into the regex used, and other information.

`bulk` consumes an iterable of documents, you are passing it a single document which won't work. You should create a function from your code that would keep `yield`ing the data and then pass it to the `bulk` helper:
```
def generate_docs():
    ....
    yield doc

helpers.bulk(es, generate_docs(), index=index_name, doc_type='logs')
```

Alternatively you can explore using logstash that has support for both reading from S3 and parsing log lines out of the box -  https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/logstash/current/index.html

Hope this helps.

Few comments:

- [ ] Can we rename activity-logs to activity-log (singular) -- we describe it as a single log.

In this we simply assume they have the JSON ready to go. Maybe we can ask for those arguments and build the JSON based off of them like we do in PowerShell?
- [ ] event-categories isn't a very helpful command -- it's mainly in our API for the Portal. We might consider removing it.
- [ ] az monitor log-profiles create -- the 'parameters' argument doesn't make sense as required to me. If you've used the storage-account-id and/or service-bus-rule-id, then you have no need for that parameters argument.
- [ ] az monitor metrics list -- the 'metric-names' argument is not listed as required, but it actually is.
- [ ] az monitor service-diagnostic-settings create -- per the above, this should just be 'diagnostic settings'. Also, can we break out the metrics and logs JSON arguments into regular arguments. - [ ] tenant-activity-logs -- we should just remove this from the CLI. The issue is that getting permissions to have this API call actually work is a bit tricky, since it's RBAC at the tenant level, so for most people it will return an auth error. It's also not used frequently.

That's not going to work for generalized vm images

To use a generalized vm image, you have to specify a vm_image_name and a system_configuration_set (to give it admin name+password, computer name, etc).  Your code change means that the system config would be ignored.

In your scenario, you should pass None for system_configuration_set and network_configuration_set.  I don't think we prevent that, but if we do, I think that's what we would have to fix.
Links is fixed and brings correctly to https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/python/api/overview/azure/datafactory?view=azure-python now

So originally we also struggled with the issue of many Python packages vs. one rpm package which is why we opened the other issue and it took us a while to wrap our head around how to approach this. We finally decided to basically follow the Python package strategy.

So what we have today are 2 packages https://build.opensuse.org/project/show/Cloud:Tools?search=azure, python-azure-sdk and python-azure-sdk-storage. As the whole thing gets broken into smaller pieces with different upstream teams managing different code streams I see the argument about the coordinated release problem.

Also note that we decided to pull our sources  from GitHub rather than pypi. We did struggle with the way things are pushed to pypi and found pulling from GitHub to be a better approach for us for package creation.

To a certain degree we/I followed a similar approach with the ec2utils we provide in our Enceladus project, https://github.com/SUSE/Enceladus/tree/master/ec2utils, meaning different release cycles for each utility.

Having different Python packages, which for us will eventually translate into different rpms implies that client code, for us azurectl, https://github.com/SUSE/azurectl, can be more precise about dependencies, which is an advantage. We have not yet packaged the new az tools, thus I cannot speak to the effect on that packaging effort and dependency management from that point of view, but I am certain we'll solve that in a reasonable way.

While I share the concern of @Fale regarding package proliferation as well as the multi Python package approach not being "Pythonic", there are equally valid arguments on the other side, meaning to have a sdk-storage package, maybe one for networking etc. I think the argument about being "Pythonic" mostly comes into play after install. Meaning as long as I can

from azure.storage import ....
from azure.networkig import ...

as a Python developer I really do not care whether site-package/azure/storage and sit-packages/azure/networking were installed by 2 distro packages or 1.  Having multiple packages may be a bit more cumbersome for the developer to set up the system, meaning the developer has to potentially install many packages but that can be easily done with a one liner:

pckgmgr --search python-azure | grep sdk | xargs pckmgr install

or a meta-package, i.e. we can easily create a python-azure-sdk-all package or create a pattern that pulls all the other packages. 

I think it is equally valid to look at each service Azure provides as a separate target and have a separate SDK for that target as it is to look at the API as a whole.

That it was decided at  the origination of boto, and now carried into botocore, that all of the AWS API should be in one SDK, one Python package, is just as valid a decision as the decision made here that each service should have its own SDK managed by separate teams.

So long story short, from my perspective either way is fine. If from a development perspective things are managed more easily at Msft to have multiple Python packages we are game to follow that route.

Re-reading this from the future, and I want to add some clarification to this comment that prompted closing:

> I think we have basically solved this in JupyterLab

JupyterLab does not solve this. I think it would be more accurate to say that JuptyerLab has instead committed to *not* solving this issue. The JupyterLab approach to extensions makes solving these target use cases that prompted this issue more difficult to impossible:

- installing a kernel package (not in the server env) wants to deliver the required js (at a minimum, requires runtime-loaded js)
- two kernels require incompatible versions of the same extension, e.g. myextension@2.0 and myextension@1.0 (requires being able to load different versions of the same library in different notebooks, which is *possible* via nbextensions if installed with a version in the path, but impossible in JupyterLab, in my understanding, due to the monolithic app bundle)

Instead, I would say that JupyterLab draws a more explicit line, that kernel packages and frontend packages are fundamentally separate, and to install a tool that has both frontend and backend components will always require two separate, explicit installation steps (which may be encapsulated in a single metapackage install in the common case where the kernel and server are in the same env). I think the JupyterLab position is that kernel packages should never be able to deliver javascript to the frontend, and instead choose to communicate with mime-types and protocols. There are plenty of reasons for working this way, but we shouldn't claim to have a solution to this issue.

It sounds like you're trying an import in two different installations of Python, or two different environments. Check `sys.executable` to see which Python and environment you're running in, and `sys.path` to see where it looks to import modules.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:

```shell
source activate myenv  # On Windows, just 'activate myenv' (no 'source')
conda install ...
```

For more detail, see [this blog post](http://takluyver.github.io/posts/i-cant-import-it.html).

Re human subjects: the document you need is here:
http://cphs.berkeley.edu/review.html

The key point I believe is that you only need to do paperwork if your
project is "designed to develop or contribute to generalizable knowledge",
i.e. if your goal is to do science and learn about how things work in
general, *not* if your goal is to study a specific entity. (I think of this
as the "would a journal care about my results" test.) They give the
specific example that if you measure how well a class learned because you
want to know if your curriculum works, then this is not "human subjects
research"; if you measure how well a class learned because you want to know
how curriculums work in general, then this is human subjects research.

In our case, we don't care about writing journal articles about general
principles for software design, we just want to know what the right thing
to do is in our specific situation. Ergo, not human subjects research.

If you want to confirm this interpretation, then there's a phone number to
call and talk to an expert, and a procedure for getting the result in
writing, given here:
  http://cphs.berkeley.edu/faqs.html#a1

For example, see stdlib logging [dictionary configuration](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/logging.config.html#logging-config-dictschema) schema.

Logging handler schema is this: 
```
The configuring dict is searched for the following keys:

class (mandatory). This is the fully qualified name of the handler class.
level (optional). The level of the handler.
formatter (optional). The id of the formatter for this handler.
filters (optional). A list of ids of the filters for this handler.
All other keys are passed through as keyword arguments to the handler’s constructor.
```
So there are some well-defined fields that could be easily described and validated as a `Model`, and any other fields are explicitly allowed. But it is the property of a given type, not the whole schema, so setting `strict=False` does not solve this. Extra fields in the other parts of the schema are forbidden (if not allowed explicitly there).

Whether it is a property of a `Model` or a `ModelType` that references it, I don't know. Both seem to fit here.

I think `PolyModelType` is a fine name, because in most use cases it is used with a root class and its descendants and aims at taking advantage of polymorphism in a classic OOP way. "member" types in a `PolyModelType` often form a tree structure, while structure of those of a union type is flat.

I do think we can eventually deprecate the use of [passing an iterable of types](https://github.com/schematics/schematics/blob/dac37186be514b6091f451e8ca6eb24778515378/schematics/types/compound.py#L321-L324) to `PolyModelType` and move those use cases to `UnionType`, i.e. write `UnionType([TruckType(), BoatType()])` instead of `PolyModelType([TruckType(), BoatType()])`.

I totally agree.

The options code currently seems to special-case members starting with "_", but it doesn't actually set them on the hidden `_options` object:

```python
>>> class Foo(Model):
...     class Options:
...         _test = 123
... 
>>> Foo.Options._test
123
>>> Foo._options._test
AttributeError
```

I believe the original intent is just to skip `__special__` attributes. But we could build on this and allow custom members beginning with an underscore and only skip those that are *actually* special.

@galgalesh This is interesting. One worry I have is around compatibility between charms.reactive 1 and 2, but I'll write that up (hopefully) in another issue.

The thing I like from what @galgalesh proposes is that the conditions stated on the handler are still true when the handler is eventually run.  This, I feel, is important from a charm author perspective as I think it would be a surprise if this wasn't the case (i.e. the conditions are written above the function)!

I don't think we should enforce order of handler execution as that would be difficult to determine in a charm with several of layers.  It might be implementation dependent on the order in which reactive walks the filesystem, and thus imports the modules, and hence the order might be arbitrary?  Thus, we probably shouldn't make this a pre-condition for this feature/topic.

### A slightly different take on the proposal (this is still a work in progress)

So I'd like to declare the queue dead too.  (Sort of).  Instead we might have:

* A list of all possible handlers
* A list of handlers that have been run

Then:

1. Evaluate the conditions for the first handler in the list.  If true, then run the handlers and then add it to the handlers that have been run list.
2. Continue until the end of the list.
3. Start back at 1 with the remaining handlers that haven't run.
4. Exit if no handlers were run during a pass through the remaining list.

Consequences:

a. A handler will at most be run only once.
b. The conditions on the handler will always be true when the handler is run.

Issues:

a. A handler will at most be run only once.
b. The order is indeterminate.
c. There is a hard to debug bug lurking.

The bug is:

```python
@when('flag1')
def funca():
    remove_state('flag1')

@when('flag1')
def funcb():
    # do something
```

i.e. depending on the order of the functions, 'do something' may or may not happen.  I think that this is a error in writing the charm.  Perhaps we can flag this during charm build to indicate that two functions have exactly the same conditions?

I think there are other issues with this, but I wanted to add it to the discussion to see if it provokes further thought in the feature.,

If I understood it correctly, the logic for puppetdb < 4.1.0 is:

On noop runs:
  - failed: something quite unhappy happened here, like a wrong resource status test
  - noop: run with pending items 
  - unchanged: run without pending items 


On regular runs:
  - failed: run with a failure 
  - changed: run with only successes 
  - unchanged: run without changes attempted


So the ambiguous state was 'unchanged', not really 'noop'. What happened is that 'unchanged' is not ambiguous any more, but now 'noop' is (it can mean either no changes pending or changes pending for noop runs). As the applications using this library cannot tell which version of puppetdb is running, it's hard to tell if 'noop' status can mean only pending changes or if it's the ambiguous one which means two things. 


I think it's important to maintain the consistence for consumers of the API, regardless of the version of puppetdb we are using. My opinion is that we should keep 'noop' status for only pending changes, and keep 'unchanged' ambiguous as the first fix. I'm happy to do this change. 

About improvements, I suppose renaming 'noop' to 'pending_changes' could be a good step, but I'd assume this is a breaking change. Also, it's possible to split 'unchanged' between 'unchanged' and 'unchanged_noop' or something.

The state of the device can be discovered by querying the following REST endpoint:
"/mgmt/tm/shared/bigip-failover-state"
As far as licensing goes, the information on licensed modules and licensed state can be found under:
"/mgmt/tm/shared/licensing/registration"
There is also another Endpoint that deals with license activation state (that would be probably useful to check the progress of license install.
This is the endpoint: "/mgmt/tm/shared/licensing/activation"

Manual for an MVC application:
1. Create a new MVC app with the latest SDK and via the command line: `dotnet new mvc` on a windows laptop/pc
2. Change the cproj file with the following:
```
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>netcoreapp2.0</TargetFramework>
    <MvcRazorCompileOnPublish>false</MvcRazorCompileOnPublish>
  <RuntimeIdentifiers>win-arm;linux-arm</RuntimeIdentifiers>
    <UserSecretsId>aspnet-test6-1765DAD7-51F4-4572-8443-7F7BD9581173</UserSecretsId>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.AspNetCore.All" Version="2.0.0-preview2-25159" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <DotNetCliToolReference Include="Microsoft.VisualStudio.Web.CodeGeneration.Tools" Version="2.0.0-preview2-25159" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>


```
3. run `dotnet restore`
4. run dotnet publish -r linux-arm
5. copy the output in the pubished folder (bin\Debug\netcoreapp2.0\linux-arm\publish) to a folder on your raspberry pi (in my case raspbian)
6. go to the folder by ssh and run: `sudo ./yourprojectname`

Tada! A working MVC application on your raspberry pi!

Your symlinks might not match where Homebrew put your libcrypto and libssl.

The quick check is

```
otool -L /usr/local/lib/libcrypto.1.0.0.dylib
otool -L /usr/local/lib/libssl.1.0.0.dylib
```

With a bad symlink `otool -L` will report `No such file or directory`.  If both libraries print their dependencies successfully then you've run into a unique version of the problem :smile:.  Assuming one, or both, fails, check the target of the symlink (e.g. /usr/local/opt/openssl/lib/libcrypto.1.0.0.dylib) to see what's up. Well, this is silly. pep8 fails with `E402 module level import not at top of file`. We're allowed to do `try: import theano; except: raise Something` before importing all the lasagne submodules, but we're neither allowed to do `if not hasattr(theano....)`, nor are we allowed to define a string variable for the long text message that's to be shared between "you don't have Theano, please do ..." and "your Theano is too old, please do ...".

Shall we disable E402 for `__init__.py`, or move the `if ...` check to the end and duplicate the string? We can't move the `try: import theano` to the end because we need to run that before importing any submodule.
A fix was provided for Theano and the problem is solved for me.

> We'd need to check if that's the case.

Okay, float32 scalars are not turned into tensors and copied over (would have been silly in the first place anyway):
```
In [1]: import theano
Using gpu device 0: GeForce GT 640 (CNMeM is disabled)

In[2]: T = theano.tensor

In [3]: x = T.matrix()

In [4]: s = x.shape[0]

In [5]: theano.printing.debugprint(theano.function([s], (s / 1.5).astype('int64')))
Elemwise{Composite{Cast{int64}((i0 * i1))}} [@A] ''   0
 |TensorConstant{0.666666666667} [@B]
 |<TensorType(int64, scalar)> [@C]

In [6]: theano.printing.debugprint(theano.function([s], (s.astype('float32') / 1.5).astype('int64')))
Elemwise{Composite{Cast{int64}((i0 * Cast{float32}(i1)))}} [@A] ''   0
 |TensorConstant{0.666666686535} [@B]
 |<TensorType(int64, scalar)> [@C]
```
Note that `/ 1.5` gets turned into a multiplication with two thirds, but for the second case (that casts the shape to float32 first), the two thirds are less precise, because they're single precision. Fun.

When the downsample factor is an integer, the float32 cast makes things a little ugly:
```
In [22]: theano.printing.debugprint(theano.function([s], (s / 2).astype('int64')))                    
Elemwise{int_div,no_inplace} [@A] ''   0
 |<TensorType(int64, scalar)> [@B]
 |TensorConstant{2} [@C]

In [23]: theano.printing.debugprint(theano.function([s], (s.astype('float32') / 2).astype('int64')))
Elemwise{Composite{Cast{int64}((i0 * Cast{float32}(i1)))}} [@A] ''   0
 |TensorConstant{0.5} [@B]
 |<TensorType(int64, scalar)> [@C]
```
The float32 cast turns a simple integer division into a floating point multiplication with two type casts. Probably that won't ever lead to numerical problems with usual input sizes and sampling factors, but I'm leaning towards keeping things as they are. If we get reports of users getting confused by the float64 warning, we can think about merging it. (Unless anybody else has a different opinion?)

> @317070 Would operators really change that much?
Well, the main argument I read here, is that there is worry on creating confusion between Theano, Numpy and Lasagne objects. Retrospectively, I think the exact reason I'd like these operations to be there, is because I already am confused on a regular basis. I often need to go back in the code because I treated lasagna layers as Theano tensors. Or needed to create new layers for trivial tasks:
https://github.com/317070/kaggle-heart/blob/master/deep_learning_layers.py#L268
https://github.com/317070/kaggle-heart/blob/master/layers.py#L77
https://github.com/317070/kaggle-heart/blob/master/layers.py#L90
https://github.com/317070/kaggle-heart/blob/master/layers.py#L350
(although, this is code from 8 months ago. Some of them might already be easier to do)

It makes the code more verbose and harder to read. I always find Polish notation difficult to process. I still have some np.dot() nightmare code laying around.

So for me, adding these operators would resolve some of the confusion by effectively doing what I expected it to do on the point of confusion. So, I would argue the confusion is already there, and it might be better to mitigate the problem, rather than fight it.

> @317070 Would operators really change that much?
Well, no. They are after all operators which will do something you can already do. It's that I regularly catch myself trying to use these operators, instead of the more verbose current approach. I think that's an indication it might be beneficial to allow the use of these operators instead.

> It looks like this is just max-pooling with a pool size dependent on the input, so that the output always has the same size (e.g. 7x7)? That should be fairly simple to implement in pure Theano. And since it's unlikely to be a very time-consuming part of a network, making a faster C implementation probably isn't worth it.

But the input of ROIPooling layer in a batch is several object proposal sub-windows of the same image. They have different sizes and all max-pooling to the same size (e.g. 7x7) with different pooling sizes. So i think it's not very easy to implement.

An aside:

[Properties are an association of keys to values in objects](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Working_with_Objects). They're what you'd call attributes with Python objects. To make matters worse (better?), HTML uses the term attributes.

```html

```
a = document.createElement('a');
a.href = "https://nteract.io"; // href is a property that becomes an attribute on the page

I committed the new cleaner solution.

Now a user can define the 'edit_handler' as usual (no more edit_handler_set needed). The user can also define more custom panels in the 'edit_handler' and these will be also patched by the wagtail modeltranslation.

Sample usage:
```
class BlogIndexPage(Page):

    bar = RichTextField(blank = True)
    foo = RichTextField(blank = True)

    content_panels = ....
    bar = [FieldPanel('bar')]

    edit_handler = TabbedInterface([
        ObjectList(content_panels, heading = 'Content'),
        ObjectList(Page.promote_panels, heading = 'Promote'),
        ObjectList(Page.settings_panels, heading = 'Settings'), classname = "settings"),
        ObjectList(bar, heading = 'Bar')), #A custom tab panel referenced by a method attribute
        ObjectList([FieldPanel('foo')], heading = 'Foo')), #A custom tab panel inline created
    ])

```

wagtail modeltransaltion, after patching a tab panel, ovverrides the attribute referencing it. That is:
the attribute content_panels will point to the new patched tab panel. My modification does the same for the custom tab panels too and in case of a missing attribute (like the Foo above) will create an attribute named "tab_panels_%d" (tab_panels_4 in this example).

So, currently this seems to work because we just cache states. Should we also consider caching exploration techniques as well? I’m not sure about what should be useful in reality... For example, we might need to cache tracer otie, at least the bb_cnt attribute.

The problem with caching techniques is that they may contain function callbacks which are not picklable. And yeah, pypy pickling has a bug as well (the import issue discussed in slack)

The breakpoint stuff is definitely not a good idea, since there's no good way to make sure that you get to tear down setup stuff that you do to each of the states you're working with.

I'm definitely not following the conversation on per-state timeouts - the only thing I think this issue is trying to address is being able to set a global timeout for the simulation manager. You can accomplish this with a signal by tracking yourself how much time has elapsed since the timer has started, and then setting a signal-based timer to wake up after `timeout - time_elapsed` seconds. I think a per-state timeout with a `timed_out` stash would be better suited for a different technique entirely.

Thanks for the suggestion. I don't really like the idea of adding this to addcomment(). Maybe this could exist as its own helper like Bug.has_comment(msg) or something but it may not even be worth handling that, I'd need to see what an implementation looks like.

Over time I've realized that adding high level features on top of the API wrapper calls is a pain in the butt. For example, in this case it hides from the user exactly what is going on, like fetching all bug comments which (depending on how you initially queried bugzilla) can mean an extra round trip, which is very slow. This encourages very slow code and increased bugzilla load, especially from automated tools. If users are forced to do the minimal dupe detection themselves, it's easier for them to detect where slowness is coming from, like the added round trip.

Somewhat related, bodhi shouldn't be using addcomment in this case, since it means one bugzilla round trip per bug (and round trips here aren't like quick http round trips, we are talking 1 second minimum for bugzilla.redhat.com XMLRPC). Instead bodhi should be using build_update/update_bugs to do a batch operation. Those APIs are very thin wrappers around the XMLRPC APIs so a checkdupes= option isn't a good fit there either IMO

While I'm usually in favor of not breaking BC, I would prefer if we give DAT a fresh start without the problems of the past (if you look at the bitbucket issues, a lot of them are related to django version compatibility).

Also, django official support for 1.4, 1.5 and 1.6 will end very soon, so I'm not sure it's worth adding a dependency.

I have found this: 
[https://pygobject.readthedocs.io/en/latest/faq.html](https://pygobject.readthedocs.io/en/latest/faq.html):

> ## Frequently Asked Questions
> ### Can I use PyGObject with virtualenv?
> 
> To use PyGObject in a virtualenv you have to install it globally and use --system-site-packages.
> 
> ```
> virtualenv --system-site-packages --python=python2 venv
> source venv/bin/activate
> python -c "import gi"
> deactivate
> 
> ```
> You can also symlink “gi” and “cairo” in the virtualenv, but this is not supported.
> ### What about the PyGObject package on PyPI?
> 
> The [PyGObject](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/PyGObject) on PyPI is the old PyGObject 2 and should not be used in new projects.
> ### Can I install PyGObject through pip somehow?
> 
> You can install directly from git:
> 
> ```
> virtualenv --system-site-packages --python=python2 venv
> source venv/bin/activate
> pip install "git+https://git.gnome.org/browse/pygobject@3.22.0"
> python -c "import gi"
> deactivate
> 
> ```
> Note that this uses autotools internally and not distutils.
>
> (...)
>
>
From https://virtualenv.pypa.io/en/stable/userguide/#the-system-site-packages-option :

> ### The `--system-site-packages` Option
> 
> If you build with `virtualenv --system-site-packages ENV`, your virtual environment will inherit packages from `/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages` (or wherever your global site-packages directory is).
> 
> This can be used if you have control over the global site-packages directory, and you want to depend on the packages there. If you want isolation from the global system, do not use this flag.
> 
>

From https://virtualenv.pypa.io/en/stable/reference/#cmdoption-system-site-packages :
>
> ###  `--system-site-packages`
> 
>     Give the virtual environment access to the global site-packages.
> 
>
From https://virtualenv.pypa.io/en/stable/reference/#cmdoption-p :
>
> ### `-p PYTHON_EXE`, `--python=PYTHON_EXE`
> 
>     The Python interpreter to use, e.g., –python=python2.5 will use the python2.5 interpreter to create the new environment. The default is the interpreter that virtualenv was installed with (like `/usr/bin/python`)
>

The browser automatically adds `If-None-Match` and `If-Modified-Since` based on the first responses. The server returns 304 and the client reuses the former response.

I could fix the fiddle with this hack:
```js
  return fetch(base + path, {
  	headers: {
      Authorization: "Basic " + btoa(`${user}:${pass}`),
      "If-None-Match": "",
      "If-Modified-Since": "",
    }
  })
```

I read the specs:
- **13.4 Response Cacheability** 
https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec13.html#sec13.4
- **14.9.1 What is Cacheable**
https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec14.html#sec14.8

I felt dumb, I don't understand 100% of it.

As @almet recommended, I digged in Gecko source code. And found [the piece of code that is responsible for the behaviour that we see](https://dxr.mozilla.org/mozilla-central/rev/46fe2115d46a5bb40523b8466341d8f9a26e1bdf/netwerk/protocol/http/nsHttpChannel.cpp#3608-3658)

I could understand that the client tries to «validate» its cache with those `If-Last-Modified` and `If-None-Match`. In our case, the server returns 304 (ie. no new object created meanwhile) , so the client thinks it can re-use its cache!


## `Cache-Control: no-store` should do it.

Then, when my eyes had burnt, I found a very useful page, by Google, which makes it very clear:
https://developers.google.com/web/fundamentals/performance/optimizing-content-efficiency/http-caching

> “no-cache” indicates that the returned response cannot be used to satisfy a subsequent request to the same URL without first checking with the server if the response has changed. As a result, if a proper validation token (ETag) is present, no-cache will incur a roundtrip to validate the cached response, but can eliminate the download if the resource has not changed.

> By contrast, “no-store” is much simpler, as it simply disallows the browser and all intermediate caches to store any version of the returned response - e.g. one containing private personal or banking data. Everytime the user requests this asset, a request is sent to the server and a full response is downloaded each and every time.

I came back to the spec. This is still... opaque.

> 14.9.2 What May be Stored by Caches
> 
> no-store
>    The purpose of the no-store directive is to prevent the inadvertent release or retention of sensitive information (for example, **on backup tapes**). [...]

> I could very easily do a quick efficient version of this using numpy and do a pull request -- just was wondering whether or not it would be worthwhile.

Yes, go head to make a PR for it. The code should have unittest too. Thanks and cheers.

Hi Dorodnic, this case is also on the Intel Support forums.  The user agreed with your suggestion that using rs2_project_point_to_pixel was the way to go.  I wasn't able to provide an explanation for how to do this though.

The full discussion on Intel Support is here:

https://communities.intel.com/message/558449#558449

You must have the calibration data of the camera that was used to take these images in order to successfully align the images - i.e the intrinsics and extrinsics data.

If you have it, please continue to read. 
If not- I'm not sure I have an idea how to can be done, possibly `opencv` has a solution for that.

I'll just write down what can be done and let me know what you are missing or don't understand...

There are a few ways to make what you want happen:
1. Create a `rs2::software_device` (not sure if we have this in python already), add the correct calibration data to it. Then, use it to load images from your data base and use `align.process` to align each frameset.
This will provide you with a robust way of using `align.process` and also allow you to use these frames as if they are live streams, and also use any other functionality of the SDK with this devices.
2.  Use the "free functions" of projection: `rs2_deproject_pixel_to_point`, `rs2_transform_point_to_point`, `rs2_project_point_to_pixel` which are available on python. Using this way you should simply iterate each pixel in the depth frame and transform it to get the matching color pixel. This is basically what `align.process` is doing but you will have to implement it yourself.

Normally we use Tornado `@gen.coroutine`s and the `yield` statement.  Search for `@gen_cluster` tests in https://github.com/dask/distributed/blob/master/distributed/tests/test_client.py and take a look at http://distributed.readthedocs.io/en/latest/develop.html#writing-tests .

I have a command tool that uses cmd2 with a CmdLoop() for standard cli type
operations, primarily to execute a particular query command, call it "tql",
and surrounding configuration commands to set up a suitable environment for
tql. cache management, cache persistence, bunch of config/status command to
on-process query results etc. with command history and all the trimmings.
It works great!

I also need to be able to use this in a one-shot mode however, where
somebody just has the tool to run a single tql query and then exit back to
the shell. Not least because this tool ends up being integrated into a
splunk query in some incarnations in this sort of one-shot mode.

To make this nice, I provide some switches to run 'load foo' and 'load bar'
type cmd2 commands before and after the one-shot to pre-load and post-save
the query environment. Which is done by one or more onecmd_plus_hooks
before and after then main "do the tql thing" onecmd_plus_hook. Which all
works smashingly, except for the load command, if I use 0.7.5 and older. So
I don't call it in an event loop, but depending on
tool-command-line-switches, I might do 0-2 calls prior to the 'main' query
oneccmd_plus_hook, and one or two after.

I also want to be able to use my Command() class as a an API for others to
use, where similar "do this before/after" would also be either built-in to
extra functions I add to my version of Command() or folks could call
directly. In that instance one might well argue that the
onecommand_plus_hook calls would be being called in some form of
event-loop, as there is no control over how the 3rd party tool might want
to drive this in order to get access to this tql environment and its
results. However, I'd look at it more as a one-shot, when whoever needs to
run a tql thing, kind of operation. That is as a sub-system sub-query type
of operational mode, rather than being included into the main event loop
'every time around' sort of thing.

Having onecommand_plus_hooks calling _cmdloop() would not be ideal. There
is too much back/forth relationship between those two.
If the requirements for 3rd party frameworks or custom commandloops permit
do_load() to call _commandLoop() however, as I hinted at in my request,
that would solve the matter of seemingly lost entries on the commandqueue
in this case. I did put that in as stated to test, and while it fails a
bunch of tests in the unit-test framework, for my purposes that would just
work. But yes, I don't know enough of what else it might break doing that,
otherwise I might in fact have done a PR myself :)

Tom

I'm not sure I fully understand the use case here.  From pybind's point of view, the holder is of secondary importance (and there are some cases, for example a pointer returned from a function defined with `py::return_value_policy::reference` where the holder is never constructed at all). The pointer itself, however, always matters, and we implicitly assume that a successfully constructed Python wrapper always has a valid instance pointer.

As for dealing with a class that isn't default constructible, you can do either of:

```C++
py::class_<C>(m, "C")
    .def(py::init<int, int>()) // Wraps a public C(int, int) constructor
    // Construct the instance some other way:
    .def(py::init([](double a, double b) { return new C(a, b); }))
```
to expose whatever sort of constructor you want to Python.  (The latter version will also let you return a holder, though the holder needs to actually hold an instance for it to work).

This looks like a regression to me, this used to work way back (e.g. in v1.2).

The intent of this feature was that a function returning a function doesn't need a return value policy (not a ``class_<>`` type), and that this flag could be forwarded to be used as the return value policy of the returned function so that the user has a way of customizing this behavior, e.g.
```cpp
m.def("return_a_function", &function_returning_a_std_function, return_value_policy::take_ownership);
```

I'm not sure if the following ever worked, but theoretically it should also be possible to use ``py::cast<>`` to do this in other situations, e.g.

```cpp
std::function<..> f = ...;
py::function func = py::cast(f, return_value_policy::take_ownership);
```
The pybind11 documentation is really spotty here, I don't think we even document the return value policy argument of ``py::cast``.

I don't like the characterization of `normal` vs. `advanced`, but we could certainly make using Certbot in this way easier.

`certbot --webroot --installer apache` is currently the most succinct way to do what you want (use the `webroot` plugin for authentication and the `Apache` plugin for installation). We could potentially shorten this just down to just `certbot --webroot`, but the thought was that if people are manually specifying `webroot`, we're not sure whether or not they want to use `Apache` for automatically installation.

We could also potentially add support for the `http-01` challenge (the one that `webroot` uses) to the Apache plugin. We could even make it the preferred challenge type for Apache to get around these issues, but we'd have to do a lot of testing to make sure we're not causing more problems with this change.
