Thanks for sharing your feedback. I'll start by saying the `manual` plugin certainly needs some UI work. In many places, the plugin is completely hidden from the user because of this.

> 1) I agreed to this and the tool keeps prompting me for every (sub)domain I have on the list.
> Why?

A fix for this has landed in `master` and will be included in the next Certbot release.

> 2) When a certificate doesn't verify prompt the user again and not just fail.

This is a bit tricky. Certbot uses an open protocol called [ACME](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-02) to get your certificates. There is a fair amount of negotiation that happens between the client and server before you get to the step of setting up challenges. If you fail a challenge, most of that work goes out the window and we have to start from scratch. We could potentially initiate another request with the server for you, but in most cases, failure to complete a challenge is the symptom of a larger problem that wouldn't be solved by trying again.

> 3) If you try to use --nginx this fails miserably so I have used manual mode

The Nginx plugin isn't included in [certbot](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#nginx)-[auto](https://github.com/certbot/certbot#current-features).

> 4) there has to be an option that would generate a list of files so I don't have to manually create those.

Depending on your setup, [webroot](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#webroot) may be what you want.

I think it depends on your setup whether automatically creating the files or providing you with the contents of the files is easier. We could potentially add an option to dump all the files in a directory for the user, however, because the files must have the names specified by the certificate authority which uses a long base64 string, conveying which file is for which domain is a bit tricky (see [the spec](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-02#section-7.2)). (Technically, the files just need to be accessible at a certain URI, however, in most setups, this is effectively the same thing). I suppose we could dump all the files in a directory with their long, unintelligible names and print a table showing which file is for which domain in the terminal? I'm not convinced this is that useful personally.

> 5) There has to be a better process for authenticating subdomains. The files should end in .txt so they can be easily downloaded.

The process for authenticating subdomains is controlled by the ACME spec which is in the hands of the IETF and outside of the scope of Certbot (which simply follows the spec). Feel free to make suggestions on their [repo](https://github.com/ietf-wg-acme/acme) or [mailing list](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/browse/acme/).

> 6) the output of the manual file should also be .crt and key files and not just a bunch of .json files.

Can you tell me which files you are referring to? Do you mean the files in `/etc/letsencrypt/account`? These files have little to no use for the average user and are generated by Certbot for later runs of the program.

Your certificates and keys are saved in `/etc/letsencrypt/live/<domain>`. If you really want to use the files in `/etc/letsencrypt/account` (which does not contain a certificate so we can't include a `.crt` file), can you describe to me your use case?

As a first step, you should define a recursive function which takes a node and determines whether that node represents a sequence of operations that *always* has an explicit return instruction. Here are some cases:

1. If the node is just a single statement, check whether it's a return statement.
2. If it's a sequence of statements, check whether one of them is a return statement.
3. If it's an if statement, check *each branch* separately, and only return True if *every* branch has an explicit return.
4. If it's a loop, check the body of the loop.

I think this might be down to the OGC "standard" definition of polygon validity. If I'm remembering correctly, it's allowed for an interior ring to touch, but not cross, an exterior ring once. But it's not okay for a ring (exterior or interior) to touch or cross itself.

However, the [latest version of the Mapbox vector tile specification](https://github.com/mapbox/vector-tile-spec/tree/master/2.1) says:

> Linear rings MUST be geometric objects that have no anomalous geometric points, such as self-intersection or self-tangency. ... Polygon geometries MUST NOT have any interior rings that intersect and interior rings MUST be enclosed by the exterior ring.

The first sentence would (like the OGC standard), appear to exclude the first polygon with the self-tangential exterior ring. It's not clear to me whether the second sentence would also exclude the second polygon with the tangential interior ring. The [documentation](https://www.mapbox.com/vector-tiles/specification/#winding-order) does show an example with clearly overlapping exterior and interior, though!

Cool. Sure yes you are right about the zoom.. was just using an example :). I use transforms in my own code which apply to both input and target images (segmentation) so I definitely understand that issue..  The way I do it is to have the target image as an optional argument:

e.g.

```python
def random_transform(x, y=None):
    # build up sub-transforms
    if random_shift:
         random_shift_matrix = ..affine transform matrix..
    if random_rotation:
         ...
    # combine sub-transforms into a single transform for one interpolation
    final_transform = np.ones(3,3)
    for sub_transform in sub_transforms:
         final_transform = np.dot(final_transform, sub_transform)
    
    # apply transform to x 
    x = apply_transform(x, final_transform)
    
    # apply to y if necessary
    if y:
        y = apply_transform(y, final_transform)
    
    return x, y
```

 > You have to patch some files and move specific directories

Well, I messed around with that idea, this is what I did: 
1. Backed up directory "Streamlink" first. 
2. I fetched [streamlink-master.zip](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/archive/master.zip) and unzipped it.
3. I used folders and files from within "streamlink-master" directory to overwrite 
existing folders and files inside original "streamlink" directory, making sure 
the directory structure/hierarchy is kept intact.
4. After a bit of trial-and-error, I discovered that for RTMP plugins (like itvplayer) 
to function correctly, I had to use file "rtmpdump.py" from your original distribution... 

And a tip for other Windows users: 
Instead of launching the Command Prompt and CD-ing to the directory where the package 
resides (or browsing to the directory in Explorer, keeping SHIFT pressed, right-clicking 
the directory and from the context menu selecting "Open Command Window Here"), 
create inside the directory a windows shortcut file (hidden extension .lnk) named 
"Run_StramlinkPortable", its target field should read: 
`C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe /k Streamlink.bat` 
and its "Start in:" field should be left empty. DC the shortcut and you should be fine! 
(Tested on VistaSP2 & 7SP1 only!).

Hi @pfalcon! Can you give more details on the problems you ran into with
lack of fairness in uasyncio? (Interesting project by the way, I hadn't
encountered it before.) I read the linked bug report but couldn't figure
out what exactly the problem was, or what degree of unfairness the prior
algorithm allowed.

To make sure it's clear: trio's current scheduling algorithm is randomized,
but is still careful to put a strict bound on worst-case unfairness. If you
have two tasks executing 'await sleep(0)' in a loop, then strict FIFO would
give you the schedule ABABABAB... Trio doesn't guarantee that, but the
worst that the current scheduler would allow is ABBAABBAABB... (and in
practice since it's randomized you'll get something in between those two
schedules – it's easy to try this with a print in the loop and see what
happens).

Thanks for the clarification! Makes perfect sense to me. I support the
change.

Any other part of the API that still doesn’t use the same pattern? I don’t
remember any.

```
     "executor"   : #{Description: Define the executor used as task executor,
                    # Type : String,

                    # Required : True},
```

> I actually think we could get both 1+2 in the following manner. If the serializer returns a list/tuple, send all element as message frames. Otherwise just send the result. On the deserialization side, if there is one message frame, pass that to the deserializer as is, otherwise pass a list of frames. Do you think that will work?

Hm, not really.  It would only work if you require that a list-returning serializer never returns a list of length 1.  Also, the more black-box approach should probably be passed *both* args and kwargs, rather than getting them separately, because how are you going to determine how many frames should be passed to the args deserializer, and how many should be passed for kwargs?

> On the errback: what should the errback get passed? Also, how do you think we should pass the original exception to the RemoteRPCException so the user to figure out what went wrong?

Probably the same thing we do in IPython - ename, evalue, traceback as strs.

> Not sure how to handle failures. Would you integrate heartbeating? What would that API look like on the client side of things?

I would skip heartbeats, and just use timeouts.  This would mean requests would need to discard replies with the wrong msg_id, as they would be stale replies from timed out requests.
